title: Needle-felted mouse set
subtitle: Three field mice in graduated greys, 5-6 cm tall, with pink ears and thread tails
excerpt: Three field mice needle-felted in graduated grey merino, each 5-6 cm tall, with tiny pink ears and a fine thread tail. The bodies are round and compact, built from grey merino stabbed over a small core. Ears and tail are attached last, once the body is fully shaped.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from traditional needle-felting practice. The three-graduated-sizes form draws from a long English folk-craft tradition of cottage-figure groupings, common in Victorian and Edwardian needle-and-cloth animal figures.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of an individual fibre in a fleece or roving, measured from tip to cut end. Short-staple wools (such as merino at 6-9 cm) felt quickly and produce a dense, smooth surface. Long-staple wools felt more slowly and produce a slightly coarser surface. Staple length affects how much felting time and agitation is needed to compact the fibres together.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: Three field mice in graduated greys make a compact project for someone new to needle felting. The bodies are simple round-bottomed ovals, each requiring only basic stabbing to build the form. The short

of merino means the fibres lock together quickly, making it a practical first choice for small figures. The three mice graduate from about 5 cm (smallest) to 6 cm (largest), each in a different shade of grey merino: pale grey through to charcoal. Pink merino forms the small cupped ears; a length of fine cotton thread or a strand of fine-ply yarn makes the tail. Ears and tail go on only after the body is fully compacted and shaped.

items.name: Light pink merino roving (ears for all three)
Fine cotton thread or fine-ply yarn in pale pink or cream (tails)
Palm guard or folded kitchen sponge

content.text: Work each mouse body separately, starting with the largest. Take a palm-sized clump of charcoal grey merino and roll it into a teardrop shape: one end slightly rounded (the rump), the other end slightly narrowed and pointed (the nose). The largest mouse body should measure about 4.5 cm long before compacting. Place it on the foam mat and stab with the 38-gauge needle, rotating it steadily so all sides compact evenly. Stab for 5-7 minutes until the teardrop holds its shape firmly and does not spring back when pressed. Repeat for the middle mouse (mid grey, body 3.5 cm long) and the smallest mouse (pale grey, body 2.5 cm long). The three finished bodies should sit flat on the mat with a stable flat base.

Switch to the 40-gauge needle and work over the entire surface of each body with short, close strokes to smooth out any loose surface fibres and even out any bumps. Pay attention to the nose end: stab around the very tip to define the pointed muzzle clearly. The surface should feel uniformly firm all over. Pull a tiny wisp of the same colour merino and lay it over any thin or patchy area, then stab it in with the 40-gauge needle. Stand each body upright on the mat to check the base sits flat; if it rocks, stab down through the base on the high side to even it out.

Pinch a very small amount of pink merino for each ear. The ears are tiny: each pinch should be roughly the size of a lentil for the smallest mouse and a small pea for the largest. Place each pinch flat on the foam mat and stab it lightly with the 40-gauge needle, leaving the base of the ear loose and unfirmed. Fold the base of the ear over slightly to create a small cupped shape, then hold the fold in place with a finger and stab through it twice to lock the fold. The ear should look like a tiny rounded cup, slightly concave on the front face. Make two ears per mouse, six ears in total.

Position each pair of ears at the top of the head, one on each side, roughly at the widest point of the skull. Hold an ear against the body at the correct angle and stab through the loose base of the ear into the body with the 38-gauge needle. Use 6-8 firm strokes to lock the ear fibres into the body. The ear should stand upright without drooping. Repeat for the second ear, checking that both ears sit at the same height and the same outward angle from the sides of the head.

Cut the cotton thread or fine-ply yarn into three lengths: 8 cm for the largest mouse, 7 cm for the middle, 6 cm for the smallest. Thread one end of each tail length 1-2 cm into the rump end of the matching body, pushing it in with the tip of the 40-gauge needle used as a guide. Once the thread end is tucked in, stab directly over it several times to lock the fibres around it. The tail should hold firmly under a gentle tug. Let the thread hang free from the rump; do not needle the tail itself.

items.problem: The ears droop or fall away from the head
items.cause: The ear base was too compacted before attaching, so the needle could not push enough fibres from the ear into the body
items.fix: Leave the base of each ear loose before attachment. Stab through the loose base into the body rather than trying to attach a fully felted ear to the surface.

items.cause: The thread end was not inserted deeply enough or was not secured with sufficient stabbing
items.fix: Push the thread end at least 1.5 cm into the rump and stab firmly over the entry point at least 10 times. Use the needle to work a few wisps of body-colour merino over the entry point to lock the thread end under the surface.

items.problem: The three bodies look very similar in size
items.cause: The pre-compaction sizes were not different enough
items.fix: The compaction ratio for merino is roughly 20-25 percent. Start with clearly different sizes of raw roving clumps and check the finished compacted sizes against each other before adding ears and tails.

content.text: Brush each mouse gently with a dry soft-bristle brush to remove any loose surface fibres. Do not machine-wash needle-felted figures. If the figures accumulate dust over time, a light brush is sufficient. Store in a sealed container or cloth bag away from direct sunlight and away from wool moths, which will damage the merino fibres.
